1. Open the **Data** toolbox in Rguroo.  
2. From the [Data Import]{.dpd} dropdown, select [Dataset Repository]{.fun}.  
3. In the top search box, type [kozak]{.typein}, then select the [Statistics Using Technology – Kozak]{.repo} repository.  
4. In the middle search box, type the first few letters of the dataset, and choose your dataset that appears in the lower panel.

7. To view the dataset, double-click the dataset under the **Data** toolbox list.

In chapter 1, you were introduced to the concepts of population, which again is a collection of all the individuals of interest. Remember, in most cases you can’t access the entire population, so you have to take a sample.  Once you collect that sample, you often end up with a large number of data values. What can you do with them? No one likes to look at just a set of numbers. One option is to organize the data into a table or a graph. Ultimately, you want to be able to use that table or graph to interpret the data, to describe the distribution of the data, and to explore different characteristics of the data.

This chapter will focus on organizing data using tables, creating graphs of the data, and using the tables and graphs to understand various characteristics of the data. 

This textbook uses Rguroo to perform all graphical and descriptive statistics, and all statistical inferences. When using Rguroo, you will need to either import a dataset from a repository or create a dataset. For directions on how to create a dataset in Rguroo, please refer to Chapter 0.

```{r setup2, include=FALSE,echo=FALSE}
library("mosaicCore")
library("mosaic")
#library("NHANES")
library("Lock5Data")
library("MASS")
```


Remember, categorical (qualitative) data are words describing a characteristic of the individual. Once you’ve gathered raw data, the real work begins. A long list of observations doesn’t tell you much on its own. Raw data are usually messy, unstructured, and difficult to understand or use. The next step is to organize the data in a way that reveals patterns and meaning. 

### Organizing Categorical Data

A simple way to organize categorical data is to create a table such as a frequency distribution or a relative frequency distribution. Both of these organize non‑numerical categories into groups and show how often each category occurs.

A **frequency distribution for categorical data** is a table that lists each category along with its corresponding frequency.

A **relative frequency distribution for categorical data** is a table that lists each category along with its corresponding relative frequency. We calculate relative frequencies by dividing the frequency (count) of each category by the total number of observations. Relative frequencies can be expressed in the form of a reduced fraction, proportion (decimal), or percentage.

These displays of categorical data help you quickly see which categories are most common, how the data is spread across different groups, and whether any categories stand out as unusually frequent or rare.

::: {#exm-creating-a-frequency-and-relative-frequency-distribution}
## Creating a Frequency and Relative Frequency Distribution

Data was collected for two semesters in a statistics class on students’ transportation, demographics, living expenses, preferences, and academic majors.  Create a frequency distribution and relative frequency distribution for [vehicle]{.var} type.

![Rguroo dataset logo](Rguroo_Icons/Rguroo_repo_logo.png) The dataset for this example is available in the Rguroo dataset repository [Kozak]{.repo}, with the dataset name [class_survey1]{.data}. @tbl-Class shows the variables and the first 6 rows of the data set. A complete description of the variables is provided in the [dataset code book](#code-book-class) that follows.


:::

```{r class-data1-table,echo=FALSE}

#| label: tbl-Class
#| tbl-cap: "Class Survey"
Class <- read.csv("https://krkozak.github.io/MAT160/class_survey.csv")

```

### Graphical Displays of Categorical Data

Another way to display data is with a graph. There are several different graphs that are used for categorical data. Typical categorical graphs include bar graphs, Pareto charts, and pie charts.  

**Bar graphs or barplots** consist of the frequencies or relative frequencies on one axis and the categories on the other axis. Drawing the barplot using Rguroo is performed by importing the dataset (or creating a dataset) and using the **Plots** toolbox in Rguroo.

::: {#exm-drawing-a-bar-chart}

Another version of a barplot that is useful is a relative frequency barplot. A relative frequency barplot provides a clear way to compare categories by showing the proportion (or percentage) of observations in each category, which is especially useful when comparing datasets of different sizes.  By converting counts into proportions, the graph allows us to compare categories based on their relative sizes rather than their raw counts. This can make differences between categories easier to see, especially when the total number of observations differs across datasets. As a result, relative frequency barplots make it easier to compare groups and clearly communicate those comparisons to others.

Rguroo can generate a relative frequency bar graph from the same [Barplot]{.dialog} in the **Plot** toolbox.

::: {#exm-drawing-a-relative-frequency-bar-chart}

The beauty of datasets with multiple variables is that you can answer many questions from the data. Suppose you want to see if gender makes a difference for the type of car a person drives. @fig-class_data_bar_gender is a barplot that compares vehicle type by gender. We see that Ford is the most common vehicle in this sample and is used only by females. Chevrolet ranks second and is used equally by males and females. Jeep, Subaru, and Toyota are favored more by females than males, although the differences are small. Many other vehicle types (for example, Audi, Buick, Honda, Hyundai, Mercury, and Nissan) appear only once or twice, indicating they are less common in this sample. 

Overall, we see some differences in vehicle preferences by gender, but the counts are small, so we should be cautious about drawing strong conclusions.

Another type of graph that is also useful for categorical data is a pie chart. A pie chart is a circular graph where each slice represents a percentage of the whole, proportional to the numerical value it represents. Pie charts allow for easy visualization of how each part contributes to the whole.

::: {#exm-drawing-a-pie-chart}

**Description** The farms dataset has 20 rows and 4 columns. The rows are farms on the Dutch island of Terschelling and the columns are factors describing the management of grassland.

Format

[Manure]{.var}: Manure usage – classes C0 to C4.

Source
J.C. Gower and D.J. Hand (1996) Biplots. Chapman & Hall, Table 4.6.

Quoted from:
R.H.G. Jongman, C.J.F. ter Braak and O.F.R. van Tongeren (1987) Data Analysis in Community and Landscape Ecology. PUDOC, Wageningen.

References
Venables, W. N. and Ripley, B. D. (2002) Modern Applied Statistics with S. Fourth edition. Springer.

## Numerical Data

As with numerical (quantitative) data, it is also advantageous to organize numerical data since a long list of raw numbers  usually does not provide insight into the  patterns or important characteristics of a sample or population. 

### Organizing Numerical Data

Frequency and relative frequency distributions can also be constructed for numerical data. In this case, the categories are numerical values that occur in the data with their corresponding frequency counts or proportions. However, many numerical variables contain many distinct values, so listing every individual value in a regular frequency or relative frequency distribution is usually not meaningful. 

For numerical data that are widely spread, we can group the data values into classes, also known as binning. When you group data into classes/bins, you create a **grouped frequency distribution**.

Lower and upper class limits define the boundaries of each class in a grouped frequency distribution. The **lower class limit** is the smallest value that can belong to a class, while the **upper class limit** is the largest value that can belong to that same class. Together, these limits specify exactly which data values fall into each interval. Each class has a **class width**, which is the difference between the lower and upper class limits. 

Most of the time, classes are displayed using interval notation. For example, suppose we want to group the ages of students into a group from 20-25. This could be displayed using interval notation, [20,25). This interval would include every student whose age is 20 to 25 years, including 20 years old, but not 25 years old. These limits keep the classes organized, prevent overlap, and ensure every data value has a clear place within the distribution. In this example, the class width is $25-20=5$. 

Rguroo has three built-in methods to calculate the number of bins. However, we will use Freedman–Diaconis method it takes into account both how much data we have and how spread out the data are. This helps create bins that better represent messy real-world data and data that may contain outliers.  In addition to the built-in methods, you can manually specify the bin settings in Rguroo if you want more control over the bins. 

::: {#exm-grouped-frequency-distribution}

### Graphical Displays of Numerical Data

There are several different graphs we can use to display numerical data. These graphs help us see how the numerical values are spread out and how they vary. When we describe how the values are arranged or spread out, we are describing the distribution of the data. From these graphs, we can identify important features of the distribution, such as its shape, center, and spread. The following are a few common graphs for numerical data: 

**Histogram:**  The graphical counterpart to a frequency or relative frequency table for continuous numerical data. A histogram is a graph with frequencies (counts) or relative frequencies (proportions) on the vertical axis and classes on the horizontal axis. Each class is represented by a rectangle. The height of each rectangle shows the frequency (or relative frequency), and the width of the rectangle is the class width.  For a given data range, choosing more bins produces narrower class widths, while choosing fewer bins produces wider class widths. Histograms are generally used for continuous data.  

**Density Plot:** A graph similar to a histogram, but instead of using bars, it displays a smooth curve to represent the distribution of the data. The curve is created by smoothing the data using advanced statistical methods.  We will use Rguroo to perform this smoothing for us. Because a density plot does not use bins, it is not affected by the choice of the number of bins. As a result, it often provides a clearer view of the overall shape of the distribution. 

**Stem and Leaf Plot:**  A graph, used primarily for smaller datasets, that organizes numerical data by splitting each value into a “stem” (the leading digit or digits) and a “leaf” (the last digit). This plot allows us  to see the distribution, frequency, and shape  of the data while still preserving the original data values (possibly in some rounded form).

When we look at data, some values may stand out from the others. An **outlier** is a data value that lies far from the rest of the values in a data set. Because outliers can strongly affect summaries and graphs, they should always be investigated to determine whether they represent a data entry or measurement error, or a genuine but unusual observation.


::: {#exm-drawing-a-histogram-density-plot}

### Shapes of the Distribution

When you examine  a distribution, begin by looking at its overall shape. The most common shapes are **bell-shape**, **skewed** (to the right or to the left), and **uniform**. Although, some distributions may not follow a clear pattern. Another shape of interest occurs when a graph has more than one peak; we call this a multimodal shape. 

Modality refers to the number of peaks in a distribution. A **unimodal** distribution has one peak, and a **bimodal** distribution has two peaks. A distribution with more than two peaks is called **multimodal**. Usually, if a graph has more than two peaks, it suggests that the data come from multiple groups with different characteristics related to the measured variable. 

Other important features to consider are how spread out are the data, and where the center of the graph is. These concepts will be explored in Chapter 3.

### Examples of Graphs

**Bell-Shape Distribution**

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "See the description of the graph below the graph"
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-bell_shape
#| fig-cap: "Bell Shape Graph"
#| out.width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics(
  "Rguroo_outputs/Graphical_Description_of_Data/symmetric.png"
)
```


@fig-bell_shape is a bell-shaped histogram.  Bell shaped graphs have a balanced, mirror‑like appearance, with the bars rising to a peak near the center and then decreasing at roughly the same rate on both sides. The left and right halves of the distribution look similar in both height and spread, so if you folded the graph down the middle, the two sides would line up closely. There are no long tails pulling the shape in one direction, and the overall pattern often forms a smooth, mound‑shaped curve. Note that bell-shape doesn’t require perfect symmetry.

**Skewed Right Distribution**

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "See the description of the graph below the graph"
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-skewed_right
#| fig-cap: "Skewed Right Graph"
#| out.width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics(
  "Rguroo_outputs/Graphical_Description_of_Data/skewed_right.png"
)
```


@fig-skewed_right is a skewed right histogram that has most of its highest bars on the left side, with the heights tapering off gradually as you move to the right. The bulk of the data forms a tall block on the left, while a long, stretched‑out tail extends as you move right. The most frequently occurring data sits closer to the left, and the right tail is noticeably longer and thinner than the left.

**Skewed Left Distribution**

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "See the description of the graph below the graph"
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-skewed_left
#| fig-cap: "Skewed Left Graph"
#| out.width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics(
  "Rguroo_outputs/Graphical_Description_of_Data/skewed_left2.png"
)
```


@fig-skewed_left is a skewed left histogram that has most of its highest bars on the right side, with the heights tapering off gradually as you move to the left. The bulk of the data forms a tall block on the right, while a long, stretched‑out tail extends as you move left.The most frequently occurring data sits closer to the right, and the left tail is noticeably longer and thinner than the left.

**Bimodal Distribution**

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "See the description of the graph below the graph"
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-bimodal
#| fig-cap: "Bimodal Graph"
#| out.width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics(
  "Rguroo_outputs/Graphical_Description_of_Data/bimodal_symmetric.png"
)
```


@fig-bimodal is a bimodal histogram which has two distinct peaks, meaning the bars rise to form one cluster of high values, dip down in the middle, and then rise again to form a second cluster.  Visually, the histogram looks like a pair of hills or humps separated by a noticeable valley. In this histogram, the overall shape is symmetric; however, this is not a requirement for a bimodal graph. In addition, the separation between the peaks can be sharp or gradual.

**Uniform Distribution**

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "See the description of the graph below the graph"
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-uniform
#| fig-cap: "Uniform Graph"
#| out.width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics(
  "Rguroo_outputs/Graphical_Description_of_Data/uniform.png"
)
```


@fig-uniform is a uniform histogram where the bars are roughly the same height, giving the graph a flat and even appearance across the entire range of values. No single interval stands out as especially common or rare; instead, the data are spread evenly, with each bar occurring at about the same frequency. Visually, the graph looks like a row of nearly level rectangles, without peaks, clusters. Note that the graph above is perfectly uniform (all bar heights are equal); however, a distribution does not need to have exactly equal bar heights to be considered uniform. As long as the bars are approximately the same height, with no clear peaks or areas that stand out as noticeably higher or lower than the others, the distribution can be described as uniform.

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-tuition_data_density_type
#| fig-cap: "Instate Tuition at all Four Year institutions"
#| out.width: "90%"
knitr::include_graphics(
  "Rguroo_outputs/Graphical_Description_of_Data/density_tuition_instate_type_2.svg"

```

@fig-tuition_data_density_type  shows density plots for in-state tuitionfor forprofit, private nonprofit, and public 4year colleges. The for-profit and public 4-year colleges both have peaks on the lower end of tuition, while the private nonprofit colleges have a peak in the middle of the distribution at a much higher tuition level. Among the three groups, public 4-year colleges have the highest peak, indicating the greatest concentration of schools in a narrow tuition range, followed by for-profit colleges, with private nonprofit colleges having the lowest peak. Because these are density plots, the height of each curve reflects how concentrated the data are within certain tuition ranges, and the shape of the curve shows where most institutions fall. 

The density plots reveal distinct tuition patterns across the three types of 4year institutions. Public colleges have the lowest instate tuition, with most schools clustering around $10,000 and relatively little variation, indicating that public tuition is generally affordable and fairly consistent nationwide. Forprofit colleges fall in the middle, with tuition centered near $20,000 and a moderate spread, suggesting that many of these institutions charge similar amounts, although a few are more expensive.  

Private nonprofit colleges stand out as the most costly group, with a peak around $35,000 and a wide distribution that extends to much higher tuition levels. This broad spread shows that private colleges vary substantially in price, with some moderately priced and others extremely expensive. Comparing the density curves by type of institution makes it clear that public colleges are the most affordable, forprofit colleges occupy a middle ground, and private nonprofit colleges are the most expensive and most variable in their tuition levels.

## Other Graphical Representations of Data

There are many other types of graphs. Some of the more common ones are the scatterplot, and a time series plot. There are also many different graphs that have emerged lately for categorical and numerical data. Many are found in publications and websites.  

**Boxplots** are another graphical display for numerical data that allow us to compare the distributions of two or more groups. They summarize important features of the data, such as the median, variability, and potential outliers. Because boxplots are based on specific numerical summaries, we will develop those ideas in the next chapter before constructing and interpreting boxplots. 

### Scatterplots

Sometimes you have two numerical variables, and you want to see if they are related. A scatterplot helps you visualize the relationship between the two variables. A scatterplot displays ordered pairs on a coordinate system, with one variable plotted on the horizontal (x) axis and the other on the vertical (y) axis.

::: {#exm-scatter-plot}

**Need Example**

:::


### **Time-Series Plot**

A time series plot is a graph showing numerical data in chronological order. The horizontal axis represents time, and the vertical axis represents the measured variable. For example, a time series plot could be used to show housing prices over the last five years. 

The purpose of a time-series plot is to look for patterns or trends over time. However, we must be cautious when interpreting trends. For example, just because you observe an increase does not mean the increase will continue indefinitely. As an example, prior to 2007, many people noticed that housing prices were increasing. The belief at the time was that housing prices would continue to increase. However, the housing bubble burst in 2007, and many houses lost value. 

::: {#exm-time-series}
## Creating a Time Series Plot

Data was collected from a simulation study of motorcycle accidents designed to test crash helmets. The study recorded the acceleration of the rider’s head immediately after impact. Head acceleration measures how quickly the speed of the head changes during the crash and is measured in $g$ units where $1g = 9.8 m/s^2$ meters per second squared. Create a time series plot of the head acceleration versus time.

## Code book for Mcycle Dataset

**Description:** A dataset giving a series of measurements of head acceleration in a simulated motorcycle accident, used to test crash helmets.

Format

This dataset contains the following columns:

[times]{.var}: The time after impact measured in milliseconds.

[accel]{.var}: Head acceleration measured in gravitational acceleration (g).

Source
Silverman, B. W. (1985) Some aspects of the spline smoothing approach to non-parametric curve fitting. Journal of the Royal Statistical Society series B 47, 1–52.

References
Venables, W. N. and Ripley, B. D. (1999) Modern Applied Statistics with S-PLUS. Third Edition. Springer.

:::


:::{.solution}

First, we identify which variable will be placed on each axis. Time after impact (in milliseconds) will be placed on the horizontal axis because it moves forward in time and helps us see how things change. Head acceleration (in g’s) will be placed on the vertical axis because it is the value we are measuring at each moment after the impact. We can use the scatterplot graph to create the time series plot.

Click to expand the box below see how to create a time series plot (using a scaterplot) of head acceleration versus time.

:::: {.callout-note appearance="simple" collapse="true" icon="none" title="![](Rguroo_Icons/R_circle_logo.svg){width=22px style='vertical-align:middle;'} Creating Time Series Plot in Rguroo"}

**Before you begin:** Make sure you have already imported the [mcycle]{.data} dataset into your Rguroo account, as was shown [here](#Importing-Data-to-Rguroo-Chapter-2).

1. Open the **Plots** toolbox in Rguroo.  
2. Open the [Create Plot]{.dpd} dropdown, and select [Scatterplot]{.fun}. This opens the Scatterplot dialog.  
3. In the Scatterplot dialog, choose the [mcycle]{.data} dataset from the [Dataset]{.dpd} dropdown.  
4. Choose the [times]{.var} variable from the [Predictor (x)]{.dpd} dropdown. 
5. Choose the [accel]{.var} variable from the [Response (y)]{.dpd} dropdown.
6. In the **Label** section of the dialog:  
   - Enter [Scatterplot of Head Acceleration vs. Time After Impact]{.typein} in the [Title]{.des} textbox.  
   - Enter [Time After Impact (milliseconds)]{.typein} in the [X-Axis]{.des} textbox.  
   - Enter [Head Acceleration (g)]{.typein} in the [Y-Axis]{.des} textbox.  
6. Click the preview icon ![preview icon](Rguroo_Icons/preview_inline.png) to see a preview of the scatterplot.

::: {.callout-tip .inner-callout appearance="simple" collapse="true" icon="none" title="Click here to see the Rguroo dialog"}
![Time Seires dialog showing settings for creating time series plot of ADI totals.](Rguroo_dialogs/Graphical_Description_of_Data/dialog_scatterplot_mycycle.png){width="500px"}
:::

::::


```{r weight-bp-sys-data, echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "See the description of the graph below the graph"
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-mcycle
#| fig-cap: "Time versus Head Acceleration"
knitr::include_graphics(
  "Rguroo_outputs/Graphical_Description_of_Data/scatterplot_mcycle.svg"
)
```

@fig-mcycle shows how head acceleration changes after an impact. At first, the acceleration stays close to zero, meaning the head hasn’t begun to move yet. Around 20 milliseconds, there is a sudden and dramatic drop to about –120 g, indicating a rapid backward motion caused by the initial force of the impact. Immediately afterward, the acceleration rebounds sharply upward to more than +60 g, showing the head snapping forward. Following this, the acceleration values fluctuate between negative and positive g's, but gradually settle toward zero. This suggests that the head’s motion is slowing down and returning to a stable state. Overall, the pattern reflects a typical impact response: an initial delay, a strong backward acceleration, a forward rebound, and then diminishing movement over time. 

:::

In chapter 1, you were introduced to the concepts of population, which again is a collection of all the individuals of interest. Remember, in most cases you can’t access the entire population, so you have to take a sample.  Once you collect that sample, you often end up with a large number of data values. What can you do with them? No one likes to look at just a set of numbers. One option is to organize the data into a table or a graph. Ultimately, you want to be able to use that table or graph to interpret the data, to describe the distribution of the data, and to explore different characteristics of the data.

This chapter will focus on organizing data using tables, creating graphs of the data, and using the tables and graphs to understand various characteristics of the data. 

This textbook uses Rguroo to perform all graphical and descriptive statistics, and all statistical inferences. When using Rguroo, you will need to either import a dataset from a repository or create a dataset. For directions on how to create a dataset in Rguroo, please refer to Chapter 0.

```{r setup2, include=FALSE,echo=FALSE}
library("mosaicCore")
library("mosaic")
#library("NHANES")
library("Lock5Data")
library("MASS")
```

## Categorical Data

Remember, categorical (qualitative) data are words describing a characteristic of the individual. Once you’ve gathered raw data, the real work begins. A long list of observations doesn’t tell you much on its own. Raw data are usually messy, unstructured, and difficult to understand or use. The next step is to organize the data in a way that reveals patterns and meaning. 

### Organizing Categorical Data

A simple way to organize categorical data is to create a table such as a frequency distribution or a relative frequency distribution. Both of these organize non‑numerical categories into groups and show how often each category occurs.

A **frequency distribution for categorical data** is a table that lists each category along with its corresponding frequency.

A **relative frequency distribution for categorical data** is a table that lists each category along with its corresponding relative frequency. We calculate relative frequencies by dividing the frequency (count) of each category by the total number of observations. Relative frequencies can be expressed in the form of a reduced fraction, proportion (decimal), or percentage.

These displays of categorical data help you quickly see which categories are most common, how the data is spread across different groups, and whether any categories stand out as unusually frequent or rare.

::: {#exm-creating-a-frequency-and-relative-frequency-distribution}
## Creating a Frequency and Relative Frequency Distribution

### Graphical Displays of Categorical Data

Another way to display data is with a graph. There are several different graphs that are used for categorical data. Typical categorical graphs include bar graphs, Pareto charts, and pie charts.  

**Bar graphs or barplots** consist of the frequencies or relative frequencies on one axis and the categories on the other axis. Drawing the barplot using Rguroo is performed by importing the dataset (or creating a dataset) and using the **Plots** toolbox in Rguroo.

::: {#exm-drawing-a-bar-chart}

**All graphs should have labels on each axis and a title for the graph.**

Another version of a barplot that is useful is a relative frequency barplot. A relative frequency barplot provides a clear way to compare categories by showing the proportion (or percentage) of observations in each category, which is especially useful when comparing datasets of different sizes.  By converting counts into proportions, the graph allows us to compare categories based on their relative sizes rather than their raw counts. This can make differences between categories easier to see, especially when the total number of observations differs across datasets. As a result, relative frequency barplots make it easier to compare groups and clearly communicate those comparisons to others.

Rguroo can generate a relative frequency bar graph from the same [Barplot]{.dialog} in the **Plot** toolbox.

::: {#exm-drawing-a-relative-frequency-bar-chart}

## Numerical Data

As with numerical (quantitative) data, it is also advantageous to organize numerical data since a long list of raw numbers  usually does not provide insight into the  patterns or important characteristics of a sample or population. 

### Organizing Numerical Data

Frequency and relative frequency distributions can also be constructed for numerical data. In this case, the categories are numerical values that occur in the data with their corresponding frequency counts or proportions. However, many numerical variables contain many distinct values, so listing every individual value in a regular frequency or relative frequency distribution is usually not meaningful. 

For numerical data that are widely spread, we can group the data values into classes, also known as binning. When you group data into classes/bins, you create a **grouped frequency distribution**.

Lower and upper class limits define the boundaries of each class in a grouped frequency distribution. The **lower class limit** is the smallest value that can belong to a class, while the **upper class limit** is the largest value that can belong to that same class. Together, these limits specify exactly which data values fall into each interval. Each class has a **class width**, which is the difference between the lower and upper class limits. 

Most of the time, classes are displayed using interval notation. For example, suppose we want to group the ages of students into a group from 20-25. This could be displayed using interval notation, [20,25). This interval would include every student whose age is 20 to 25 years, including 20 years old, but not 25 years old. These limits keep the classes organized, prevent overlap, and ensure every data value has a clear place within the distribution. In this example, the class width is $25-20=5$. 

Rguroo has three built-in methods to calculate the number of bins. However, we will use Freedman–Diaconis method it takes into account both how much data we have and how spread out the data are. This helps create bins that better represent messy real-world data and data that may contain outliers.  In addition to the built-in methods, you can manually specify the bin settings in Rguroo if you want more control over the bins. 

::: {#exm-grouped-frequency-distribution}

### Graphical Displays of Numerical Data

There are several different graphs we can use to display numerical data. These graphs help us see how the numerical values are spread out and how they vary. When we describe how the values are arranged or spread out, we are describing the distribution of the data. From these graphs, we can identify important features of the distribution, such as its shape, center, and spread. The following are a few common graphs for numerical data: 

**Histogram:**  The graphical counterpart to a frequency or relative frequency table for continuous numerical data. A histogram is a graph with frequencies (counts) or relative frequencies (proportions) on the vertical axis and classes on the horizontal axis. Each class is represented by a rectangle. The height of each rectangle shows the frequency (or relative frequency), and the width of the rectangle is the class width.  For a given data range, choosing more bins produces narrower class widths, while choosing fewer bins produces wider class widths. Histograms are generally used for continuous data.  

**Density Plot:** A graph similar to a histogram, but instead of using bars, it displays a smooth curve to represent the distribution of the data. The curve is created by smoothing the data using advanced statistical methods.  We will use Rguroo to perform this smoothing for us. Because a density plot does not use bins, it is not affected by the choice of the number of bins. As a result, it often provides a clearer view of the overall shape of the distribution. 

**Stem and Leaf Plot:**  A graph, used primarily for smaller datasets, that organizes numerical data by splitting each value into a “stem” (the leading digit or digits) and a “leaf” (the last digit). This plot allows us  to see the distribution, frequency, and shape  of the data while still preserving the original data values (possibly in some rounded form).

When we look at data, some values may stand out from the others. An **outlier** is a data value that lies far from the rest of the values in a data set. Because outliers can strongly affect summaries and graphs, they should always be investigated to determine whether they represent a data entry or measurement error, or a genuine but unusual observation.


::: {#exm-drawing-a-histogram-density-plot}

### Shapes of the Distribution

When you examine  a distribution, begin by looking at its overall shape. The most common shapes are **bell-shape**, **skewed** (to the right or to the left), and **uniform**. Although, some distributions may not follow a clear pattern. Another shape of interest occurs when a graph has more than one peak; we call this a multimodal shape. 

Modality refers to the number of peaks in a distribution. A **unimodal** distribution has one peak, and a **bimodal** distribution has two peaks. A distribution with more than two peaks is called **multimodal**. Usually, if a graph has more than two peaks, it suggests that the data come from multiple groups with different characteristics related to the measured variable. 

Other important features to consider are how spread out are the data, and where the center of the graph is. These concepts will be explored in Chapter 3.

### Examples of Graphs

**Bell-Shape Distribution**

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "See the description of the graph below the graph"
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-bell_shape
#| fig-cap: "Bell Shape Graph"
#| out.width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics(
  "Rguroo_outputs/Graphical_Description_of_Data/symmetric.png"
)
```


@fig-bell_shape is a bell-shaped histogram.  Bell shaped graphs have a balanced, mirror‑like appearance, with the bars rising to a peak near the center and then decreasing at roughly the same rate on both sides. The left and right halves of the distribution look similar in both height and spread, so if you folded the graph down the middle, the two sides would line up closely. There are no long tails pulling the shape in one direction, and the overall pattern often forms a smooth, mound‑shaped curve. Note that bell-shape doesn’t require perfect symmetry.

@fig-bimodal is a bimodal histogram which has two distinct peaks, meaning the bars rise to form one cluster of high values, dip down in the middle, and then rise again to form a second cluster.  Visually, the histogram looks like a pair of hills or humps separated by a noticeable valley. In this histogram, the overall shape is symmetric; however, this is not a requirement for a bimodal graph. In addition, the separation between the peaks can be sharp or gradual.

**Uniform Distribution**

```{r,echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "See the description of the graph below the graph"
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-uniform
#| fig-cap: "Uniform Graph"
#| out.width: "80%"
knitr::include_graphics(
  "Rguroo_outputs/Graphical_Description_of_Data/uniform.png"
)
```


@fig-uniform is a uniform histogram where the bars are roughly the same height, giving the graph a flat and even appearance across the entire range of values. No single interval stands out as especially common or rare; instead, the data are spread evenly, with each bar occurring at about the same frequency. Visually, the graph looks like a row of nearly level rectangles, without peaks, clusters. Note that the graph above is perfectly uniform (all bar heights are equal); however, a distribution does not need to have exactly equal bar heights to be considered uniform. As long as the bars are approximately the same height, with no clear peaks or areas that stand out as noticeably higher or lower than the others, the distribution can be described as uniform.

## Other Graphical Representations of Data

There are many other types of graphs. Some of the more common ones are the scatterplot, and a time series plot. There are also many different graphs that have emerged lately for categorical and numerical data. Many are found in publications and websites.  

**Boxplots** are another graphical display for numerical data that allow us to compare the distributions of two or more groups. They summarize important features of the data, such as the median, variability, and potential outliers. Because boxplots are based on specific numerical summaries, we will develop those ideas in the next chapter before constructing and interpreting boxplots. 

### Scatterplots

Sometimes you have two numerical variables, and you want to see if they are related. A scatterplot helps you visualize the relationship between the two variables. A scatterplot displays ordered pairs on a coordinate system, with one variable plotted on the horizontal (x) axis and the other on the vertical (y) axis.

::: {#exm-scatter-plot}

**Need Example**

:::


### **Time-Series Plot**

A time series plot is a graph showing numerical data in chronological order. The horizontal axis represents time, and the vertical axis represents the measured variable. For example, a time series plot could be used to show housing prices over the last five years. 

The purpose of a time-series plot is to look for patterns or trends over time. However, we must be cautious when interpreting trends. For example, just because you observe an increase does not mean the increase will continue indefinitely. As an example, prior to 2007, many people noticed that housing prices were increasing. The belief at the time was that housing prices would continue to increase. However, the housing bubble burst in 2007, and many houses lost value. 

::: {#exm-time-series}

## Code book for Mcycle Dataset

**Description:** A dataset giving a series of measurements of head acceleration in a simulated motorcycle accident, used to test crash helmets.

Format

Source

First, we identify which variable will be placed on each axis. Time after impact (in milliseconds) will be placed on the horizontal axis because it moves forward in time and helps us see how things change. Head acceleration (in g’s) will be placed on the vertical axis because it is the value we are measuring at each moment after the impact. We can use the scatterplot graph to create the time series plot.

Click to expand the box below see how to create a time series plot (using a scaterplot) of head acceleration versus time.

```{r weight-bp-sys-data, echo=FALSE}
#| fig-alt: "See the description of the graph below the graph"
#| warning: FALSE
#| label: fig-mcycle
#| fig-cap: "Time versus Head Acceleration"
knitr::include_graphics(
  "Rguroo_outputs/Graphical_Description_of_Data/scatterplot_mcycle.svg"
)
```

@fig-mcycle shows how head acceleration changes after an impact. At first, the acceleration stays close to zero, meaning the head hasn’t begun to move yet. Around 20 milliseconds, there is a sudden and dramatic drop to about –120 g, indicating a rapid backward motion caused by the initial force of the impact. Immediately afterward, the acceleration rebounds sharply upward to more than +60 g, showing the head snapping forward. Following this, the acceleration values fluctuate between negative and positive g's, but gradually settle toward zero. This suggests that the head’s motion is slowing down and returning to a stable state. Overall, the pattern reflects a typical impact response: an initial delay, a strong backward acceleration, a forward rebound, and then diminishing movement over time. 

:::
